Wolf et al. 2007 — personality syndrome in a spatially-explicit ABM

Source: vignettes/paper-wolf2007.Rmd
paper-wolf2007.Rmd

Spatially-explicit clade interpretation of Wolf, van Doorn, Leimar & Weissing (2007) Nature 447:581–584. Wolf’s mean-field game-theoretic mechanism (life-history asset protection → boldness-aggressiveness syndrome) re-implemented in clade’s grid-based ABM, with hawk-dove pairs drawn from the Moore neighborhood and anti-predator decisions anchored to real predator presence. Implemented in 0.7.0; see inst/julia/src/modules/personality.jl.

What the original paper claims

Wolf 2007 proposes that consistent individual differences in risk-taking behaviour (animal personalities) arise from a life-history trade-off. Individuals with high future fitness expectations (much to lose) evolve to be more risk-averse than individuals with low expectations (little to lose). Across multiple risky contexts the same asset-protection logic applies, producing correlated behavioural traits — the boldness–aggressiveness syndrome.

Three specific predictions:

  1. Coexistence of life-history strategies — disruptive selection on exploration intensity x yields a stable dimorphism (some individuals x ≈ 0, others x ≈ 1). Stable when 2·f_low > f_high > 1.
  2. Within-context consistency — across contexts and over time, an individual that is bold in one anti-predator decision is bold in subsequent ones; aggressive in one hawk-dove encounter is aggressive in the next. (Repeated games per individual within a lifetime.)
  3. Cross-context correlation (the syndrome) — boldness and aggressiveness positively correlate; both negatively correlate with exploration x.

Wolf’s model is non-spatial — every game pairs randomly across the whole population and the anti-predator game is an abstract individual draw with no actual predator on a grid.

Why spatially-explicit, not Wolf-faithful?

clade is a spatially-explicit ABM. Its value-add over Wolf’s analytic mean-field model is precisely that encounters happen somewhere, between neighbouring individuals, and that the resource economy is regulated by the grid. We could implement Wolf’s model verbatim (random pairing across the population), but that would throw away clade’s spatial machinery and reproduce a model that’s already analytically solved.

The interpretation here:

  • Hawk-dove game: pair the focal between-phase agent with a random between-phase neighbour in its Moore neighborhood (default radius 1, configurable via personality_hawkdove_radius). If no qualifying neighbour, no game this tick.
  • Anti-predator game: fires only when a real predator is within Moore radius 2 of the focal agent (uses env.predator_map). The bold/shy draw on agent.boldness then determines whether the agent gets the bold-action energy bonus and incurs the bold-action mortality risk.
  • Year-1 / year-2 reproduction: triggered at fixed agent ages (wolf_year1_repro_age = 50, wolf_year2_repro_age = 100). Year-2 reproduction also kills the parent. Game payoffs accumulated during the between-phase (year1 < age < year2) fold into year-2 fecundity.

This is an extension of Wolf 2007, not a faithful reproduction. The expected differences from Wolf’s published correlations:

  • Selection signal is weaker because (a) hawk-dove encounters depend on local density rather than uniform mixing, and (b) anti-predator events depend on predator proximity, not a constant abstract risk.
  • Wolf’s (1 + α·N_i) per-resource competition denominator is replaced by clade’s grid-based spatial competition (foraging on cells, handling time, energy budget). This regulates total population but doesn’t sharply produce the year-1-vs-year-2 dimorphism Wolf reports.

The headline result 

library(clade)
library(ggplot2)

s <- wolf_personality_specs()
s$max_ticks                       <- 5000L      # ~50 generations at year2 = 100
s$random_seed                     <- 42L
s$n_agents_init                   <- 200L
s$max_agents                      <- 800L
s$personality_hawkdove_per_tick   <- 0.3
s$personality_hawkdove_radius     <- 2L
s$n_predators_init                <- 10L       # anti-predator needs real predators
s$predator_max_agents             <- 30L
env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)

Pull final-tick trait values and check the three Wolf correlations:

recs  <- env$agents
alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive), logical(1L))
ex    <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive], function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$exploration),    numeric(1L))
bo    <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive], function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$boldness),       numeric(1L))
ag    <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive], function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$aggressiveness), numeric(1L))

cor(ex, bo)   # predicted negative — high-x agents are less bold
cor(ex, ag)   # predicted negative — high-x agents are less aggressive
cor(bo, ag)   # predicted positive — boldness-aggressiveness syndrome

Typical values at this configuration (seed = 42 only):

Correlation Predicted Phase 3a (no denominator) Phase 6 (with α=0.005)
Pearson(exploration, boldness) < 0 −0.21 +0.04
Pearson(exploration, aggressiveness) < 0 −0.09 −0.01
Pearson(boldness, aggressiveness) > 0 +0.10 +0.31

This single-seed observation suggested the Phase 6 (1+α·N_i) denominator strengthened the bold-aggro syndrome from +0.10 to +0.31 — a threefold improvement. But that read does not survive multi-seed scrutiny. The next section reports the rigorous 8-seed verdict, which reclassifies all three correlations as null.

Multi-seed verification (2026-05-16)

A multi-seed regression baseline at the default wolf_personality_specs() configuration — 8 seeds × 5000 ticks, single condition — was generated by dev/audit/fidelity/paper_wolf2007.R and saved to dev/audit/fidelity/paper_wolf2007.rds. Per-seed correlations:

seed cor(bold, aggro) cor(exp, bold) cor(exp, aggro) n_alive
1 −0.370 −0.017 −0.023 402
2 +0.248 +0.013 −0.115 401
3 −0.201 +0.017 −0.226 424
4 +0.201 +0.068 +0.058 402
5 −0.331 −0.142 +0.262 422
6 +0.052 +0.032 −0.084 430
7 +0.193 −0.034 +0.014 419
8 −0.251 +0.064 −0.163 418

Aggregate across 8 seeds, with 2σ verdict ladder:

Correlation Predicted Mean ± SE t Verdict
cor(bold, aggro) > 0 (syndrome) −0.057 ± 0.091 −0.63 null
cor(exp, bold) < 0 (asset-protection) +0.000 ± 0.024 +0.01 null
cor(exp, aggro) < 0 (asset-protection) −0.034 ± 0.054 −0.64 null

The boldness-aggressiveness syndrome is null at 8 seeds. Per-seed correlations range from −0.37 (seed 1) to +0.25 (seed 2) with no systematic direction. The single-seed observation reported above (+0.31 at seed 42, which is not one of the 8 seeds in this rerun — the rerun used seeds 1–8 to match clade’s batch_seeds() default) falls inside the +0.21 to +0.25 upper tail of the 8-seed distribution but isn’t representative.

Honest reclassification: in clade’s current kernel, the spatially- explicit interpretation of Wolf 2007 does NOT robustly reproduce the personality syndrome at the 5000-tick / 8-seed scale tested here. This is consistent with the “Honest discussion of magnitudes” below (weaker selection signal from local-density encounters) but more severe than the single-seed result suggested — the verdict is null, not a small but reliable positive.

Possible explanations for the gap, in decreasing order of likelihood:

  • Seed variance dominates the signal at 5000 ticks. The SE on bold-aggro (0.091) exceeds the absolute mean of every single-seed observation except 1 and 5. Longer runs (15 000+ ticks) might resolve an equilibrium, but that’s also the regime where Wolf’s syndrome decays per McElreath 2007 — see “Critique-aware framing” below.
  • Spatial-pairing structure breaks the mean-field signal. Wolf’s result depends on every agent pairing with every other in the between-phase; clade’s Moore-neighborhood pairing produces a fundamentally different game structure. The per-strategy (1+α·N_i) denominator (Phase 6) is the only kernel knob that partially bridges the gap; tweaking the snapshot frequency or hawk-dove radius might push the signal up but should be tested explicitly.
  • Implementation issue in inst/julia/src/modules/personality.jl. Lower-likelihood but worth keeping on the list. The deterministic kernel module has been reviewed but a multi-seed null at this scale is a candidate finding for the next kernel-review pass.

The seed-42 +0.31 observation should be read as a noise-envelope landing, not a robust syndrome signal. Phase 6’s (1+α·N_i) denominator may still be doing useful work (none of the seeds produce the +0.30+ magnitude under the pre-Phase-6 codepath either) — but its benefit has to be re-evaluated against the multi- seed baseline, not the seed-42 single-shot.

Visualising the trait distribution 

trait_df <- data.frame(
  exploration    = ex,
  boldness       = bo,
  aggressiveness = ag
)

ggplot(trait_df, aes(x = exploration, y = boldness)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, colour = "#377eb8") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "#e41a1c") +
  labs(title = "Wolf 2007 prediction: high exploration → low boldness",
       subtitle = sprintf("n = %d  Pearson r = %+.3f", length(ex), cor(ex, bo)),
       x = "Exploration trait (x in Wolf's notation)",
       y = "Boldness") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12)

ggplot(trait_df, aes(x = boldness, y = aggressiveness)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, colour = "#984ea3") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "#e41a1c") +
  labs(title = "Wolf 2007 syndrome: boldness and aggressiveness positively correlate",
       subtitle = sprintf("n = %d  Pearson r = %+.3f", length(ex), cor(bo, ag)),
       x = "Boldness", y = "Aggressiveness") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12)

Honest discussion of magnitudes

Wolf 2007 reports correlations typically > 0.5 in his Fig 4 quantitative- genetics extension. clade’s spatially-explicit interpretation produces correlations of order 0.1. Why the difference?

  1. Encounter rate. Wolf’s mean-field model has every agent paired with another every game tick. clade’s neighborhood pairing depends on local density. At 200 initial agents on 30×30 = 900 cells (~22% density), an agent’s Moore-radius-2 neighborhood (24 cells) contains ~5 other agents on average — but only some of those will be in the between-phase, so actual game encounters are sparser than Wolf’s mean- field rate.

  2. The (1 + α·N_i) competition denominator (Phase 6 refinement). clade now applies Wolf’s per-strategy denominator with N_high ≈ N·mean(x) and N_low ≈ N·mean(1-x), snapshotted once per tick. This strengthened the bold-aggro syndrome from +0.10 to +0.31, but did not produce Wolf’s year-1-vs-year-2 dimorphism: the population still locks at x≈0 because the snapshot-once-per-tick approximation plus clade’s spatial competition is coarser than Wolf’s continuous per-individual evaluation. The asset-protection signs (exp ↔︎ bold, exp ↔︎ aggro) are weaker / sign-flipped in the locked equilibrium.

  3. Population dynamics. clade’s energy economy regulates carrying capacity in addition to the (1+α·N) denominator. The two interact: the energy economy already prevents runaway growth, so the denominator’s regulating role is partly redundant and the resulting selection gradient on x is weaker than in Wolf’s pure mean-field model.

These are real interpretation choices, not bugs. clade’s value is that it embeds Wolf’s mechanism in a richer biological setting; the trade-off is weaker / sign-flipped asset-protection signal even though the defining bold-aggro syndrome is preserved.

Diploid extension (Wolf 2007 Fig 4) — clade does NOT recover the syndrome

Wolf 2007 §“Genetic architecture” reports that the diploid quantitative- genetics version of his model yields stronger correlations (r ≈ 0.65) than the haploid basic model. The conventional explanation is that diploidy + Mendelian inheritance buffers selection and allows genetic variation to accumulate at the asset-protection equilibrium.

clade supports diploidy out of the box — the genome.jl meiosis logic handles ploidy = 2 — so testing this is one line in the preset:

s <- wolf_personality_specs()
s$ploidy <- 2L            # diploid; Wolf Fig 4 setting
env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)

Observed result at seed 42 (5000 ticks, otherwise identical configuration):

Correlation Predicted Haploid (Phase 6) Diploid
Pearson(exploration, boldness) < 0 +0.044 −0.075
Pearson(exploration, aggressiveness) < 0 −0.005 −0.068
Pearson(boldness, aggressiveness) > 0 +0.307 −0.003

The diploid extension produces the opposite trade-off from haploid Phase 6:

  • The bold-aggro syndrome disappears (+0.307 → −0.003). Recombination decouples the two trait expressions across generations, so the shared-asset-protection mechanism that links them in haploid mode no longer produces a correlation.
  • The asset-protection signs flip toward the right direction (−0.075, −0.068). Diploidy seems to push the population into a different equilibrium structure where exploration does trade off against risk-taking, in line with Wolf’s prediction.

Neither pattern is what Wolf reports for his analytic diploid model. Wolf’s all three correlations strengthen together; clade’s diploid extension produces a swap — losing one and gaining the others.

The most likely explanation is the same as the haploid Phase 6 trade-off: clade’s grid-based competition and one-per-cell offspring placement constrain the equilibrium population structure in ways Wolf’s mean-field model doesn’t see. Diploid recombination interacts with this spatially-imposed structure to produce a new attractor.

The test at tests/testthat/test-personality-syndrome.R asserts only that the diploid scenario completes without crashing and produces a viable population; the specific correlation magnitudes are documented here as honest findings rather than enforced.

What’s left for follow-up work

  • Per-individual (not snapshot) frequency evaluation — switch to per-agent re-computation of (1+α·N_high) / (1+α·N_low) to recover the smoothness Wolf’s continuous model assumes. Restructures apply_lifehistory_tradeoff!.
  • Spatial vs random pairing comparison — run side-by-side with personality_hawkdove_radius = 1 (clade-native) vs an alternative global-pairing implementation (Wolf-faithful) to quantify the cost of spatial restriction.
  • Sensitivity to game frequency — the personality_hawkdove_per_tick and personality_antipred_per_tick parameters control encounter rate; sweeping these would map the encounter-rate-to-syndrome-strength relationship.

Critique-aware framing

Two published critiques of Wolf 2007 are directly testable in clade. Both have now been audited with multi-seed regression baselines (2026-05-16) — and both single-seed claims that appeared to confirm the critiques are themselves contradicted at 8 seeds.

  • Massol & Crochet (2008) Nature 451:doi:10.1038/nature06744 — argue the dimorphism depends critically on β (the trade-off shape). Tested in vignette("paper-massol-crochet-2008"). The single-seed sweep suggested syndrome peaks at Wolf’s published β = 1.25 (+0.307). At 8 seeds × 5 β values, β = 1.25 is the trough (−0.077, marginal-negative); no β value produces a robust positive syndrome. Range across β shrinks from single-seed 0.39 to multi-seed 0.13. The β-sensitivity claim survives; the “peak at Wolf’s β” claim is contradicted.
  • McElreath, Luttbeg, Fogarty, Brodin & Sih (2007) Nature 450:doi:10.1038/nature06326 — argue asset protection is a negative-feedback mechanism that erodes individual differences over time. Tested in vignette("paper-mcelreath-2007"). The single-seed trajectory “+0.307 at 5 000 ticks decaying to +0.032 at 15 000” was a noise-envelope landing. At 8 seeds × 3 horizons, bold-aggro is null at every horizon (−0.075, −0.077, +0.034) — there’s no peak to decay from. Surprise positive finding: cor(exp, aggro) at 2 000 ticks PASSes negative (t = −4.12), opposite to McElreath’s “later-peak” framing.

Cumulative multi-seed verdict across all three Wolf 2007 reproductions in clade (paper-wolf2007, paper-massol-crochet-2008, paper-mcelreath-2007): the boldness-aggressiveness syndrome does NOT robustly emerge at any (β, time-horizon) combination tested. All seed-42 +0.307 single- seed observations were noise-envelope landings on the positive tail. Both critique vignettes’ apparent confirmations dissolved with the rds upgrade. One robust positive finding survives (confirmed at 16 seeds): in the McElreath horizon sweep, cor(exp, aggro) at 2 000 ticks PASSes-negative — the asset-protection signal does appear in clade’s spatial kernel, but in the asset-protection correlation rather than the syndrome correlation, and at the earliest horizon (opposite to McElreath’s “later-peak” prediction). Both the syndrome null and the early-horizon asset-protection finding are candidates for the next kernel-review pass.

References

  • Wolf, M., van Doorn, G.S., Leimar, O. & Weissing, F.J. (2007) Life-history trade-offs favour the evolution of animal personalities. Nature 447:581–584. doi:10.1038/nature05835.
  • Massol, F. & Crochet, P. (2008) Do animal personalities emerge? Nature 451 (Brief Communications Arising). doi:10.1038/nature06744.
  • McElreath, R., Luttbeg, B., Fogarty, S.P., Brodin, T. & Sih, A. (2007) Evolution of animal personalities. Nature 450 (Brief Communications Arising). doi:10.1038/nature06326.
  • Clark, C.W. (1994) Antipredator behavior and the asset-protection principle. Behavioral Ecology 5:159–170.
